critique.summary: Microsoft has blocked a registry hack that enabled native NVMe driver support on Windows 11, which previously improved NVMe SSD performance significantly. Although the registry method no longer works, users can still enable native NVMe support using a third-party tool called ViVeTool, but compatibility issues remain.
mainPoints: A registry trick that enabled native NVMe support on Windows 11 has been blocked by Microsoft in recent Insider builds.
The native NVMe driver (nvmedisk.sys) offers up to 80% higher IOPS and lower CPU usage compared to the legacy SCSI translation layer.
The registry hack originally ported a Windows Server 2025 feature to consumer Windows 11 versions 24H2 and 25H2, improving random write performance by up to 85%.
Third-party SSD management tools and BitLocker may have compatibility issues with the native NVMe driver.
Users can still enable native NVMe support using ViVeTool, a third-party utility, but it requires elevated permissions and carries the same risks.
It is unclear when Microsoft will officially roll out native NVMe support for Windows 11 25H2 and 26H2.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are consistent with publicly available information from credible technology news sources and user reports on forums. The native NVMe driver was introduced in Windows Server 2025 and was accessible via registry hacks on Windows 11, which Microsoft has since blocked. The performance improvements cited align with benchmark results reported by independent testers like Neowin and StorageReview. The mention of ViVeTool as a workaround is accurate and documented by third-party sources. The article correctly notes compatibility issues with SSD management tools and BitLocker. Overall, the article provides an accurate and factual account of the situation.
considerations: The article does not provide official statements from Microsoft explaining why the registry hack was blocked or when native NVMe support will be officially enabled for all Windows 11 users.
Potential risks and stability concerns of enabling native NVMe support via unofficial methods like ViVeTool could be elaborated further.
The impact on enterprise environments and how Microsoft plans to handle native NVMe support in future updates is not discussed.
The article could mention alternative operating systems or solutions for users dissatisfied with Microsoft's handling of NVMe support.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around Microsoft's decision to block a registry hack that enabled native NVMe driver performance on Windows 11. One commenter criticizes Microsoft for anti-consumer behavior and outdated disk I/O subsystems, while another sarcastically mocks internal Microsoft priorities.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.text:
I mentioned this in the Scott Hanselmen trying to re-allow local-accounts for Windows thread. https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47498494

It just seems so beyond-belief that Microsoft keeps having such depraved anti-consumer behavior. Maybe perhaps this was just a not-ready-yet feature folks had enabled being moved around or shuffled. But it seems just as likely Microsoft intends to keep consumers using a decade and a half years old shitty NVMe-downcast-to-SCSI layer indefinitely, to upsell folks to fancier Windows versions or gaming systems. Microsoft intends to keep Windows consumer disk access slow and bad.

As a seasoned Linux veteran & believer, it's somewhat against my interest to share this view, to try to arouse the slumbering behemoth to action. Microsoft not getting the message and doing great misservice to their users is somewhat in my interest. The status quo of Linux being far better at everything is great: gaming is already much faster on Linux, & that should be no surprise, and disk io too. Just holding my tongue and letting Microsoft make a fool for themselves with absymal performance would be ideal. But I also believe in competition, and Linux is going to start slacking off if Microsoft can't be arsed to update a disk io subsystem that was a filthy pitiful hack when they slammed it into service a dozen years ago. We all need some pressure sometimes to get off our hinds, wake the frak up, and pay some attention.

And perhaps, maybe: even Windows users don't deserve this malpractice.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed critique of Microsoft's handling of NVMe support, highlighting valid concerns about outdated technology and competition with Linux. The emotional tone is balanced by thoughtful insights.

comments.author: andrewstuart
comments.text:
“NVME?? What’s NVME got to do with anything?

It’s the Task Bar!

For goodness sake can’t you see Windows users have lost faith because they can’t move the task bar!”

(Heard in meetings all over Microsoft campus recently).
comments.analysis: This comment uses sarcasm to mock perceived misplaced priorities at Microsoft, but does not contribute factual information about the NVMe issue itself.
